item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded:
I don't really like Japanese culture

Huh? sorry for the corpse reply, but yiiiiiikes
Congratulations! This scip deserves to go well over 100. I don't really like Japanese culture but this is nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: May the FEELS be with you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As one of those poor kids whose sanity was forever rendered non-existent shaken by End of Evangelion, the fact that you named the interviewer Dr. Akagi while also putting my heart through the wringer broke me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Do not disparage
For a heart made of sushi
May still strive, still yearn.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Start: "Hey, a man made out of sushi. Damn, isn't it fun to find a wacky article every now and then?"

End: "God, FEELS".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not a yokai. The idea is that enough emotion was put into the sushi, and some of that residue feeling latched onto it all and gave it personality and feelings.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it just me or does this sound like a yōkai of some sort? /wildguessing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great! I'll look into that. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the Russian branch picks articles based on interest? If it's something that looks fun to translate or is historically/culturally significant, they'll translate it. I don't think it's based on a "must be x-months old and at least some rating before we consider" standard or anything. But it'd be best to ask the translators.

Awesome! Man, the translated Japanese poem looks nice.

1. The article points to the sushi man being created during the Edo period, hence the wasabi sculpture's shape. Young man in thirties would suit the story more.
2. Why don't you try looking that up yourself?
3. Why don't you look through common surnames of the Edo period? Also, read the article: "Her family and my master’s family were allied in artisan trade. They served the noble classes, though her house was more auspicious than his."
4. Why don't you look through common surnames of the Edo period? It's a good idea to pick something that has corresponding meaning, (i.e. maybe a floral aspect for the "sakura maiden" and maybe a surname connecting to the sea for the sushi chef).

If you're having trouble thinking of details, try reading through the article again. I tend to add in a lot of details in my work, so successive read-throughs may reveal things you didn't pick up on before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just noticed that the Russian version of this article was out. Do they read the articles and than see how long they stay on before translating them?

Also noticed an informal wiki blog with a Japanese translation of the article on Haruchi FC2.

1) Also what setting should I base the story in? 2000's or 1900's decade? Should the sushi man's creator be a sushi master who is elderly or a young man in his thirties?

2) Is there a rating system for a sushi chef? If so what would the human chef be rated.

3) Since it is a tale what family name of the girl will sound most appropriate? Also should her family be unhappy to have her marry a sushi chef or no?

4) What name should the sushi chef be?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you're not logged into wikidot, ads will display at the bottom of pages on the SCP site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: really I didn't see any advertisements. However I did see a episode of you-tubers react regarding Japanese commercials with at least one sushi commercial.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's beyond fitting that there was an ad for sushi catering at the bottom of the page when I read this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: *facepalm sorry my mistake

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An SCP discussion thread really isn't the place for this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well here's a link to first story I ever wrote online from my other forums-I-spend-time-on what do you think?

This is the link

and this is my wip

or this link

the other link

this is how so far my style of writing tales you don't have to view them all but this is just to showcase how I currently write stories.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Take your time, dude. Sushi man and I will still be here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well I'll try to get a workable draft out soon. Though I may b=not be used to how to incorporate some elements as before most of my stories have had a military
background or theme.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a good tale written about an SCP, it's in the form of a fairytale: http://www.scp-wiki.net/king-greenshield-a-fairy-tale

Maybe you can look to that as a tonal example. The subject matter you have planned out looks good, so I'm not too worried about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a story of SCP-1690's master and creator from the master's point of view and ends with him abandoning the restaurant and leaving sushi man behind. along these lines.

Since sushi man doesn't know what he lacks you know that could also be addressed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Look for things that are high-rated and similar in length to what you want to write. Also, it's up to you. What sort of story are you telling?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Any suggestions or should I just browse through them myself?"

What period or setting should the story be in? I hope to be historically correct as allowed. Not so it gets too technical but well yeah….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think so. Depending on what you want to mention in the tale, you shouldn't need to go into that many specifics.

There aren't any basic guides for tales. It's a good idea to read some of the high-rated tales to get an idea of how to write them, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The problem would be that I might have to reveal the redaction which concerns me would I have too? I'm not sure what the basic guidelines for a tale here would be as I searched the guides and there weren't any

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That would be pretty interesting. I think I'd like to read about that.

Go for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I initially considered how we go more in-depth into his personality perhaps or we go with a story of his former master attempting to please the maiden by creating sushi man which would be from the point of view of the master himself perhaps.

I might think it over more

meekly agreed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dunno, maybe. What would the separate addendum have?

I think I did. It looked a little weird to me, to be honest. The shrimp parts look too big in proportion to the body.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After much thought I decided it would be best not to mention where the soy sauce originated from for now. I wonder if this skip could have a potential separate addendum (I may draft and play around with that) like SCP-993
did. On the other hand if that doesn't work out its okay.

did you see the image someone drew of SCP-1690? it's in the fan-work thread

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…huh. No, we didn't. Maybe he cries the soy sauce? That's a bit depressing… I dunno. Yeah maybe we should just not say. ¯\(°_o)/¯

It's okay. Can't please everyone. Maybe sushi man can make the guy some dragon rolls or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait a moment what if you wrapped SCP-1690 with plastic wrap first before you hugged him? maybe that will work?

Ed edit: on the other hand would that be unnecessary cruelty? I can't decide

Ed edit: Hmmm… Zyn we never mentioned where he got the soy sauce did we? The question is should we? (<_<) (>_>)

also just looked at rating page of who rated and noticed only one minus sign I chuckled a bit but not much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've smelled worse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're going to smell like raw fish later bro

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to give it a biiiiiig hug.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmmmmm… AWCY? I'm not so sure either that they should be included it would just be awkward and probably not a up-vote addition maybe

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: *groan*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like it'd be an unnecessary GOI reference in this case, but the idea of sentimental sushi art terrorism is certainly food for thought.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
:(

This is me, giving you hate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I might get some hate from this idea, but what if the haiku ended like this:
What does my heart lack?
What do I lack?
Are we cool yet?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aww… the sushi just wants to be loved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm just happy that weeks of collaboration and drafting spearheaded by Zyn and support for this concept I came up with has endured. I'm glad I took the time to carefully think out the theory and consulting numerous admins and mods before I dared to post this.

This idea funnily came to me out of the blue it walked into my head ready-made and well… yeah. I hope another brainwave strikes soon

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's a good point. It was intended to have been a translation. I'll add that in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok you got me with the sapient sushi. The only thing that bugs me is that usually, a poem will lose its prose when translated. And since Zyn helped a hgue lot/co-wrote it, did the skip write the poem in english or is the poem can be still a poem in both languages?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Beautiful. +1

I don't even like sushi.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's definitely Zyn's feels-inducing style to this, and it's an awesome article out of a rather odd concept. +1

item.title: Re: Apology to Zyn
item.content:encoded: I'm still walking on my journey in this community and the outside world. Like sushi man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SUSHI MAN!

Thought this article was kinda cute and neat.

+1'd.

item.title: Re: Apology to Zyn
item.content:encoded:
Hey, uh… Just credit me properly in the first post. The public apology wasn't something I was trying to weasel out from you.

Again, just give proper credit where it's due.

Edit: Uh… and if you're not sure how to do that, edit your previous post using the "edit" function under the "options" tab to the lower right of every comment. Just change your comment to accurately reflect the work I did.

Edit II: Truth be told, it's a liiiittle odd-looking since the sentence "All credit goes to you for helping write-up the interview log and the poem at the end. I also gratefully thank you for rewriting my original article and for writing of her wonderful interview and the poem all of which helped breath life into this SCP" is kinda redundant since you mention the interview and the poem twice. The rest looks fine.

and um… you spelled Vivax's name wrong.

item.title: Apology to Zyn
item.content:encoded:
I formally apologize to Zyn for not properly crediting her contributions. I was unaware that I had not properly credited her involvement.

Therefore, I would now like to properly credit Zyn for rewriting my original article and her writing of the interview and the poem,

I deeply apologize for having neglectfully acknowledging your actions with only a simple "helping" you, rather than stating your co-authorship and spearheading of the piece.

I'm sorry about this violent infraction and I beg for your forgiveness Zyn and to hope that this will not overly taint future collaborations we may conduct together.

PS: I did that already hows it look?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Quite endearing. Good job you two. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dangit Zyn and Ed. Zyn, y u always do dis? You always hit me right in the feels, and props to Ed for a good concept.

Poor sushi man…

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aw, this started out almost humorous and became so touching by the end. Kudos to everybody!

item.title: Re: SCP-1690
item.content:encoded: Zyn did write the vast majority of this article, including the poem, which was the best section by far. She deserves a lot more credit than that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is great. I especially like that the sculpture is made out of material from the sushi man's heart.

Now I'm hungry for sushi, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A silly starting concept that turns into feels, with a healthy dose of Japanese ethos in the middle. Heartily approved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A sushi golem in the grip of an existential crisis? +1

It became much more interesting to me when I considered the tragedy—he's just human enough to recognize he's missing something, but may not be human enough to find it.

item.title: Re: Collaborator's Notes
item.content:encoded: D'awwww

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cuuuuuute!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is proof that execution > idea. I followed this on the draft forums for a while, and though a sushi man definitely tickled me fancy at first, I didn't think anything could be done with it.

Bravo. +1
